questions.question: A batch data-processing job can be interrupted and restarted without harm, and you want the lowest possible compute cost. Which model fits?
questions.feedback: Spot offers the deepest discount for interruptible, fault-tolerant workloads like batch processing.

questions.question: A database runs 24/7 and will for the next three years. What reduces its compute cost the most?

answers.text: A 3-year Reserved Instance or Savings Plan

questions.feedback: Steady, always-on workloads are ideal for a 1- or 3-year commitment (RIs or Savings Plans) for large discounts.

questions.question: Objects are accessed often for 30 days, then rarely. You want to cut storage cost automatically. What do you use?

answers.text: An S3 Lifecycle policy transitioning objects to a cheaper class
Delete and re-upload the objects manually each month
Move them to an EBS volume

questions.feedback: Lifecycle rules automatically transition aging objects to cheaper classes (e.g. Standard-IA, then Glacier).

questions.question: You want an alert when monthly spend is forecast to exceed a set threshold. Which tool?
questions.feedback: AWS Budgets sends alerts when actual or forecast spend crosses a threshold. Cost Explorer analyses; it doesn't alert.

questions.question: Which help reduce or control cost?

answers.text: Deleting unattached EBS volumes and old snapshots
Using CloudFront to reduce outbound data-transfer cost
Applying cost allocation tags to attribute spend
Running all workloads on Dedicated Hosts by default

questions.feedback: Clean up idle storage, cut egress with CloudFront, and tag for attribution. Dedicated Hosts are the most expensive option, not a default.
